title: Prime, Composite, or Neither
goal: Repair the idea that 1 is prime and rebuild divisor-based classification.
firstTutorQuestion: How many divisors does 1 actually have?
operatorFocus: Keep the learner on divisor count, not memorized labels.
watchFor: Learner says '1 is prime because it is special' without using divisor reasoning.
Learner can repeat 'neither' but cannot explain why.
exampleTutorMove: Ask the learner to list the divisors of 1 before naming the category.
successSignal: Learner says 1 is neither prime nor composite and gives a divisor-based reason.

title: Entry Bridge With 24
goal: Rebuild the habit of starting with an easy divisor pair instead of freezing.
firstTutorQuestion: What is the easiest divisor you can test first?
operatorFocus: Reward a calm starting move instead of speed.
watchFor: Learner freezes because they think they must finish the whole factorization at once.
Learner chooses a hard divisor first even after noticing the number is even.
exampleTutorMove: If the learner hesitates, point back to the word 'even' and ask what divisor that suggests.
successSignal: Learner chooses 2 as a starting point for 24 and explains why.

title: Rewrite the Final Factorization Cleanly
goal: Stabilize the move from split-work to a complete final prime factorization.
firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
operatorFocus: Push for one clean final line, not more splitting work.
watchFor: Learner drops a repeated factor while rewriting.
Learner leaves a composite factor in the final line.
exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and ask the learner to rebuild only the final prime-factor line.
successSignal: Learner writes one complete prime factorization with no missing or composite factor.

title: Verification Bridge With 12
goal: Teach the learner to check whether every factor is prime before accepting a factorization.
firstTutorQuestion: Before checking the product, are all the factors prime?
operatorFocus: Make 'all factors prime' come before product-checking.
watchFor: Learner accepts 2 x 6 only because it multiplies correctly.
Learner checks the product first and never inspects whether 6 is composite.
exampleTutorMove: Hold both answers side by side and ask which one still contains a composite number.
successSignal: Learner rejects 2 x 6 because 6 is not prime and chooses 2 x 2 x 3.

title: Reuse Factorization for Divisor Count
goal: Stop the learner from restarting divisor counting from scratch.
firstTutorQuestion: In this problem, do you need the factorization itself or the number of divisors?
operatorFocus: Keep the learner from listing every divisor one by one.
watchFor: Learner abandons the factorization they already built.
Learner starts listing divisors without explaining what the task is asking for.
exampleTutorMove: Ask what information the completed factorization already gives before allowing any divisor list.
successSignal: Learner starts from the existing prime factorization instead of listing divisors one by one.

title: Find the Missing Factor for a Square
goal: Connect prime-factor counts to square-number structure.
firstTutorQuestion: Which prime factor count is odd right now and needs one more?
operatorFocus: Point to odd exponent counts instead of guessing a multiplier.
watchFor: Learner picks a multiplier by trial and error.
Learner knows the answer but cannot justify it with prime-factor counts.
exampleTutorMove: Circle only the odd-count prime and ask what single factor would make that count even.
successSignal: Learner identifies the missing factor from the prime factorization and explains why.

title: Sort Common Divisor vs Common Multiple
goal: Help the learner name which structure to look for before calculating.
firstTutorQuestion: Are you looking for what overlaps or what covers both numbers?
operatorFocus: Get the learner to classify the structure before any arithmetic and keep them from computing too early.
watchFor: Learner starts calculating without deciding whether the task is GCD or LCM.
Learner uses the word 'common' but cannot distinguish shared from covering structure.
Learner classifies correctly once but then falls back into calculation-first behavior.
exampleTutorMove: Pause the learner at the first arithmetic move and ask them to restate whether this is an overlap task or a cover-both task.
successSignal: Learner names the task type and keeps calculation on hold until that structure is clear.

title: Explain Which Part of the Factorization Matters
goal: Make the learner point to the relevant part of the factorization before solving a transfer task.
firstTutorQuestion: Which part of the factorization helps you answer this question first?
operatorFocus: Make the learner point to the useful part before they compute and say why that part matters.
watchFor: Learner recites the entire factorization without selecting the relevant part.
Learner solves mechanically and only explains after the answer.
Learner points to a part but gives no reason for choosing it.
exampleTutorMove: Pause the learner and ask them to underline the part of the factorization they plan to use and say why that part answers this question.
successSignal: Learner identifies the relevant part of the factorization before using it and explains why that part is the useful one.
